A broad area of low pressure which extended from the Dakotas south into the southern Rockies brought a period of snow to a portion of northeast Montana during the evening hours of the 28th into the early morning hours of the 29th.  The bulk of the snow fell from eastern Valley County and points east to the North Dakota border.  Snowfall amounts were generally in the 2 to 4 inch range.  A few isolated spots across the Big Sheep Mountains in Dawson and southern McCone Counties had up to 6 inches of snow.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dry lightning events in August triggered wild fires in Missoula County. The Jocko Lakes Fire burned 35,850 acres and destroyed seven Out Buildings near Placid and Seeley Lake areas.

A strong cold front and upper level disturbance moved across the  central and eastern counties in the Billings County Warning Area during the late afternoon and evening hours of the 13th. Several thunderstorms developed across Northern Stillwater and Sweet Grass Counties before evolving into Supercell thunderstorms over Northern Yellowstone County. These thunderstorms then merged into a squall line across Eastern Yellowstone County and Treasure County before moving across Rosebud, Custer and Fallon Counties. Very large hail and damaging winds accompanied these thunderstorms with widespread damage reported across these areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms produced heavy rainfall in excess of 3 inches across portions of northeast Montana on June 6th into the early morning hours of the 7th.  The heavy rain led to flash flooding and flooding of small streams and creeks.  The most significant flooding was in Prairie County where Interstate 94 was under a foot of water during the late evening/early morning hours along the Prairie/Custer County border. There were also reports of flooding along Timber and Cherry Creeks in Western Prairie County where fences were washed out and gravel roads had washouts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Interstate 94 was under a foot of water along the Prairie/Custer County border.

A broad area of low pressure which extended from the Dakotas south into the southern Rockies brought a period of snow to a portion of northeast Montana during the evening hours of the 28th into the early morning hours of the 29th.  The bulk of the snow fell from eastern Valley County and points east to the North Dakota border.  Snowfall amounts were generally in the 2 to 4 inch range.  A few isolated spots across the Big Sheep Mountains in Dawson and southern McCone Counties had up to 6 inches of snow.

On December 29 and 30, moist northwesterly flow persisted over the northern Rockies. During this period, a series of shortwave disturbances moved through and produced heavy snow accumulations and strong winds across Western Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotters from Thompson Falls and Evaro reported 9.5 and 10.5 inches of new snowfall respectively. In addition, Lookout Pass reported wind gusts up to 22 mph. Pardee and Plains also reported gusty winds to 20 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On December 29 and 30, moist northwesterly flow persisted over the northern Rockies. During this period, a series of shortwave disturbances moved through and produced heavy snow accumulations and strong winds across Western Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts received from spotters and automated weather stations in this zone ranged from 5 to 20 inches. Some of the more notable amounts were 14 inches at Libby, 9 inches at Troy, 10 inches at Bull Lake and 20 inches at Grave Creek.

A complex of thunderstorms moved across Custer, Powder River and Rosebud Counties. As a result, many lightning caused fires were started during the evening of the 8th and the early morning hours of the 9th. The numerous fires were combined into what is now known as the Powder River Complex. This complex of fires burned from approximately August 8th through August 18th and consumed 23,113 acres. One mobile home and barn, as well as an abandoned school house were destroyed. (At the time of this report, damage amounts have not yet been determined.)

A large spring storm associated with a cutoff upper level low, moved across the Northern Rockies on the afternoon of June 6th through the morning of June7th. Thunderstorms developed by early afternoon across northern Wyoming and southern Montana, with heavy rainfall beginning in Sheridan, Wyoming and Carbon County, Montana at 330pm and across much of southern Montana by 6pm. By 9pm Wednesday evening, heavy rainfall became more widespread across southern Montana and continued thruogh the overnight hours. By mid morning on the 6th, rainfall had ended across the impacted area. Runoff from areas upstream of Rosebud County and the Tongue River Reservoir resulted in flooding downstream of the dam.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the late afternoon and evening of June 16, 2007, a high precipitation supercell thunderstorm tracked from across northern Montana, just to the north of a warm front.  This was the most devastating hail storm to affect the area since at least 1999, and prompted 22 severe thunderstorm and 6 tornado warnings in the NWS Glasgow county warning area. The storm began early in the day just east of Glacier National Park and gradually moved hundreds of miles eastward through the day.  By 3 pm, the storm crossed into the NWS Glasgow area in Phillips County, where it started to make a turn to the southeast.   The storm traveled for over 250 miles before decreasing in Richland County near the North Dakota border a little after 11 pm.  Hail up to baseball size and wind gusts in excess of 85 mph accompanied the storm.  Torrential heavy rainfall was also experienced, prompting seven flash flood warnings to be issued.  The large hail left a hail swath seen on GOES and Terra MODIS satellites that had a path at least 225 miles long, and up to 12 miles wide. ||The most significant damage from the storm occurred in portions of Valley and McCone Counties.  The towns of Glasgow and Nashua, both in Valley County, took a direct hit from the storm.  In Glasgow, southeast winds gusting to 60 to 65 mph ahead of the storm switched into the northwest with gusts in excess of 70 mph.  Golfball to baseball sized hail fell for several minutes before diminishing to penny sized for another 10-20 minutes.  The majority of the homes and businesses in Glasgow had some sort of damage including:  broken windows, damaged shingles, dented or broken gutters and skirting.  Many vehicles also had significant hail damage.  Just the property damage alone in Glasgow was nearly $8.0 million dollars.  As the storm continued east it hit the town of Nashua and every home and business had some form of damage from the baseball size hail and the damaging winds that occurred with it.   The winds were measured at 89 mph just west of town.  Property damage was estimated to be $3 million dollars. | |In rural areas outside of Glasgow and Nashua there were several cottonwood trees completely uprooted.  Horses and cattle were injured by the hail and wind, and Montana Fish, Wildlife and Parks reported many birds and small animals were killed by the hail.  There were thousands of acres of alfalfa, wheat, and corn that were completely destroyed.  At this time, damage estimates to crops in Valley County are at $20 million dollars.||The storm also produced heavy rainfall of 1 to 2 inches, and was followed later in the evening by additional heavy rain.  Rainfall of 2 to 7 inches fell in the Antelope, Brazil and Willow Creek basins.  The Antelope Creek basin had its most significant flood on record and one road was completely underwater for nearly a third of a mile.  One ranch was isolated by the flood waters for three days. ||As the storm continued across northern McCone County and into western Richland County it continued to produce hail up to the size of ping pong balls and wind gusts that were estimated as high as 80 to 90 mph knocked down telephone and electrical poles.   There was significant damage to farms and ranches in the Vida area, as well as significant loss of crops, estimated at $5 million dollars.  The rural nature of the area minimized the property damage that could have occurred.  ||The overall estimated damage in this storm between the hail and wind damage, and the subsequent flooding is 34.2 million dollars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two to three foot diameter cottonwood trees were uprooted.  Windows were blown out of homes.  See entire episode narrative for full details.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On January 7, a winter storm brought localized heavy snow and gusty winds to the West Glacier and Seeley Lake areas. Emergency travel only was declared by the Highway Department from Essex to over Marias pass due to heavy snow, poor visibility and blowing snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Total snowfall ranged from 6 inches in Seeley Lake to 9 inches reported by Snotel sites in the mountains.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On January 3, a winter storm system produced up to one foot of new snowfall accumulations in the mountains and four to six inches in the lower elevations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snotel data reported 6 to 12 inches of new snow above 5000 feet. Flattop Mountain reported 18 inches new snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On January 3, a winter storm system produced up to one foot of new snowfall accumulations in the mountains and four to six inches in the lower elevations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snotel data reported 10 to 12 inches of new snow above 5000 feet. Lookout Pass reported 8 to 10 inches new snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On May 21 and 22, a very strong, moist and slow moving closed low pressure system moved across Southwest Montana bringing very heavy snowfall to both the mountains and high elevated valleys. This event was a high impact event that not only caused several accidents on the highways, but caused significant tree damage as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread heavy wet snow was received across the higher valleys and mountains. Georgetown Lake received 15-22 inches, Philipsburg 16-21 inches, Avon 16-18 inches, Anaconda 7-13.5 inches, and Butte 4-6 inches of wet snow. Power outages were reported at several locations. Significant damage to trees was also reported along with several weather-realted accidents on area roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On May 21 and 22, a very strong, moist and slow moving closed low pressure system moved across Southwest Montana bringing very heavy snowfall to both the mountains and high elevated valleys. This event was a high impact event that not only caused several accidents on the highways, but caused significant tree damage as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy wet snow was received across the higher elevations of the Bitterroot and Sapphire Mountains. Snotel sites reported the following |total snowfall for the storm: Skalkaho Pass 13.0 inches, Black Pine 10.0 inches, Saddle Mountain 8.0 inches, and Twin Lakes 7.0 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful Spring upper level low slowly moved across Central Wyoming and Southeast Montana during a 48 hour period . As a result, a heavy, prolonged precipitation event occurred. The precipitation started as rain but quickly changed over to snow. The snow became heavy at times with snowfall rates of one to two inches per hour. In addition to the heavy snow, very strong northwest winds of 25 to 40 mph created blizzard conditions across portions of Southern and Southeastern Montana. Zero to near zero visibilities were common as a result of the combined heavy snow and strong winds. |The following are the many reports that were received from this major Spring blizzard: 13 inches in Biddle (2 day liquid equivalent 1.42 inches), 12 inches 14N Albion(snow total from the afternoon of the 29th through 8am on the 30th), 17 inches in Lame Deer, 24 inches with 3 foot drifts in Lodge Grass, 24 inches 1N Busby with drifts to 3 feet, 36 inches 1N Busby with cattle dying, 7 inches in Forsyth, 10 inches in Colstrip, 12 inches in Volborg(2 day liquid equivalent 1.76 inches), 10 inches 44S Ekalaka, 12 inches in Fort Smith, 6 inches 8NNE Powderville, 8 inches 8SW Biddle, 12 inches in Colstrip, 8 inches in Albion(2 day liquid equivalent 1.92 inches), 8 inches at the  Billings Airport (new daily record snowfall), 8 inches 10N Bridger, 11 inches Billings West End(8 to 11 inches reported on Rimrock Road), 8 inches 2SE Park City (5 to 8 inches with numerous 2-3 foot drifts, significant and numerous drifting across many area roads).

A Pacific cold front moved through Southern Montana. Close behind this front, an arctic front swept across the forecast area bringing bitterly cold temperatures. Snow developed across the area, with the most significant accumulations over the Beartooth/Absaroka Mountains and adjacent foothills, especially on north facing slopes. In addition to the snow, gusty winds combined with very cold temperatures created dangerous wind chills of 10 to 25 degrees below zero at times. The following snow totals were received: 10 inches 5W Red Lodge(Cole Creek Snotel), 11 inches 3SSW Luther(Burnt Mountain Snotel), 9 inches 2S Red Lodge, 6 inches in Laurel, Mc Leod, Nye, and Columbus, 9 inches in Absarokee, 8 inches in Fishtail, Emigrant, and Red Lodge Ski Area and 10 inches 1W Pryor.

A storm system moving through the region caused strong low pressure to form over Alberta. As a result, very strong winds occurred in the favored areas of the Beartooth and Absaroka Mountains. In addition, a strong cold front moved across the forecast area bringing in much cooler temperatures. The mountains experienced the heaviest snowfall late Christmas Eve into Christmas morning as a result of an unstable airmass that allowed for rapid accumulations. Windy conditions also contributed to areas of blowing and drifting snow. The following snow totals were received: 9 inches 10WSW Limestone(Placer Basin Snotel), 6 inches 2S Otter and in Otter and 8 inches 5NE Ridge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system tracked across southern Canada and produced a period of very strong wind across northeast Montana.  The strongest wind was confined to an area north of the Missouri River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This report was taken from the Plentywood Handar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system tracked across southern Canada and produced a period of very strong wind across northeast Montana.  The strongest wind was confined to an area north of the Missouri River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roof damage occurred at the dental clinic.  NWS employees later observed minor roof and siding damage to multiple residences and a small fire was reported from a downed tree arcing with a power line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous upper level storm system and strong cold front produced a widespread severe weather outbreak in northeastern Montana.  The majority of the events were high wind, a few reports of large hail, and two tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This report was taken from the Cow Creek DOT site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On November 26 and 27, a vigorous winter storm moved east across Western Montana and produced heavy snowfall amounts across both mountain and valley locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Marias Pass received 8.5 inches of new snow accumulations; Fernie received 7 inches of new snow accumulations; and Morrisey Ridge received 11 inches of new snow accumulations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On November 26 and 27, a vigorous winter storm moved east across Western Montana and produced heavy snowfall amounts across both mountain and valley locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lookout Pass and Mullan Pass received 8 and 11 inches of new snowfall accumulations, respectively. A trained weather spotter reported 6.5 inches of new snowfall accumulations four miles northwest of Noxon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On November 26 and 27, a vigorous winter storm moved east across Western Montana and produced heavy snowfall amounts across both mountain and valley locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lolo Pass received 9 inches of new snowfall accumulations. Twelve Mile creek received 12 inches of new snowfall accumulations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On November 18 and 19, a very moist Pacific storm system moved Northeast across Western Montana. The cold and moist airmass moved overtop a dome of colder air. This enhanced precipitation snowfall rates and amounts across the area. In addition to the heavy snowfall, upper level winds mixed to the surface to create breezy conditions in the Bitterroot and Sapphire mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Weather spotters across the Mission valley reported 6 to 10 inches of snowfall from this system. The heaviest amounts received were found near Saint Ignatius.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On November 18 and 19, a very moist Pacific storm system moved Northeast across Western Montana. The cold and moist airmass moved overtop a dome of colder air. This enhanced precipitation snowfall rates and amounts across the area. In addition to the heavy snowfall, upper level winds mixed to the surface to create breezy conditions in the Bitterroot and Sapphire mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts between 8 and 11 inches were recieved across the entire Missoula and Bitterroot valleys. Numerous power outages were reported in Missoula, Lolo, Stevensville, Hamilton and Florence. Power outages in Bonner and Corvallis forced school closures for the day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On November 12, a strong cold front moved across Western Montana and produced heavy snowfall and high winds in the Bitterroot and Sapphire Mountains as well as high winds in the Anaconda and Deer Lodge areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The roof was blown off the Clinton Community Church in Clinton. Portions of the roof, which was under construction, fell onto the pastor and broke his leg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On January 3, a winter storm system produced up to one foot of new snowfall accumulations in the mountains and four to six inches in the lower elevations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snotel data reported 6 to 8 inches of new snow above 6000 feet. Lolo Pass reported 7 inches new snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On January 11, extreme wind chill values of 25 to 30 degrees below zero were experienced in portions of western Montana near the Continental Divide.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway Department weather station at Essex reported wind chill reading of 22 degrees below zero between 400 am and 500 am.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On June 28, a strong short wave trough moved across Southwest Montana during the afternoon and evening hours. Isolated severe thunderstorms produced several severe downbursts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree was blown over onto the deck of a home. The tree also knocked down several powerlines as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On June 28, a strong short wave trough moved across Southwest Montana during the afternoon and evening hours. Isolated severe thunderstorms produced several severe downbursts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported an estimated wind gust to 60 mph.

A pair of intense storms moved across the area producing widespread damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous large trees and large limbs were blown down in and around Libby and Troy. In addition, a large tree was blown down on a hiker on the Cedar Creek trail 4 miles southwest of Libby. The rescue team reported that numerous trees were blown down over the trail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On December 23 and 24, another strong disturbance moved east across Western Montana and brought heavy snowfall accumulations to the high elevations and the Seeley-Swan valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lost Trail Pass and Lolo Pass received 11 inches and 8 inches of new snowfall accumulations, respectively for this event.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Seven to nine inches of heavy snowfall accumulations were received at Snotel and RAWs sites above 5600 feet. In addition, Lost Trail Pass received 8 inches of snow from this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On January 5 and 6, a strong winter storm brought heavy snow and strong gusty winds to Northwest Montana and the mountains of West Central Montana. Visibility was reduced in blowing snow over mountain passes. Total snowfall amounts ranged from 6 to 8 inches in the lower elevations to 15 inches in the mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snotel data reported 4 to 8 inches above 4000 feet, with 13 inches above 5000 feet. Local wind gusts between 20 to 30 mph were reported from automated wind sensors.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On July 6, a short wave trough moved to the northeast through Southwest Montana during the evening hours. Strong mid level winds briefly mixed to the surface as a severe thunderstorm moved across Granite county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three 6 to 8 inch diameter trees were snapped in half from Nimrod to Bearmouth.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On January 11, extreme wind chill values of 25 to 30 degrees below zero were experienced in portions of western Montana near the Continental Divide.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway Department weather stations at Avon, Helmville, and Deer Lodge reported wind chill readings of 28 to 32 degrees below zero from 100 am|to 700 am. Butte ASOS reported 24 degrees below zero at 553 am and Philipsburg RAWS site reported 27 degrees below zero at 900 am.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On May 24, a closed low pressure system dropped south from Canada along the Continental divide producing heavy snow in Glacier National Park.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Badger Pass and Flattop Mountain received 7 and 9 inches of new snowfall respectively as the Low Pressure System moved southeast along the Continental Divide.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On May 3, a deep and cold low pressure trough moved over the Northern Rockies bringing very heavy snow to the higher elevations of Southwest Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow was reported across southwest Montana, mainly during the morning hours. Snowfall ranged from 6 inches south Butte to 14 inches|around Georgetown Lake. I-90 over Homestake Pass was temporarily closed due to heavy snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On June 4, a strong cold front moved southeast across the area and produced several strong to severe thunderstorms over Western and Southwestern Montana during the late afternoon and evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe Thunderstorm downdraft winds blew several large trees down 5 miles west of Superior.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On June 4, a strong cold front moved southeast across the area and produced several strong to severe thunderstorms over Western and Southwestern Montana during the late afternoon and evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estimated Severe Thunderstorm downdraft winds of 60 mph were reported in both the Nine Mile area and in Missoula. The winds broke several large branches off of trees in Missoula.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On June 5, another frontal boundary moved southeast across the area and produced more strong to severe thunderstorms. Given colder air aloft, large hail reports were also received in addition to high wind reports.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An agrimet weather station reported a wind gust of 73 mph. In addition, numerous trees were blown down near McDonald Lake road. St. Ignatius experienced power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On June 5, another frontal boundary moved southeast across the area and produced more strong to severe thunderstorms. Given colder air aloft, large hail reports were also received in addition to high wind reports.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weather spotter reported hail stones up to one inch in diameter 6 miles north of St. Ignatius.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful Spring upper level low slowly moved across Central Wyoming and Southeast Montana during a 48 hour period . As a result, a heavy, prolonged precipitation event occurred. The precipitation started as rain but quickly changed over to snow. The snow became heavy at times with snowfall rates of one to two inches per hour. In addition to the heavy snow, very strong northwest winds of 25 to 40 mph created blizzard conditions across portions of Southern and Southeastern Montana. Zero to near zero visibilities were common as a result of the combined heavy snow and strong winds. |The following are the many reports that were received from this major Spring blizzard: 13 inches in Biddle (2 day liquid equivalent 1.42 inches), 12 inches 14N Albion(snow total from the afternoon of the 29th through 8am on the 30th), 17 inches in Lame Deer, 24 inches with 3 foot drifts in Lodge Grass, 24 inches 1N Busby with drifts to 3 feet, 36 inches 1N Busby with cattle dying, 7 inches in Forsyth, 10 inches in Colstrip, 12 inches in Volborg(2 day liquid equivalent 1.76 inches), 10 inches 44S Ekalaka, 12 inches in Fort Smith, 6 inches 8NNE Powderville, 8 inches 8SW Biddle, 12 inches in Colstrip, 8 inches in Albion(2 day liquid equivalent 1.92 inches), 8 inches at the  Billings Airport (new daily record snowfall), 8 inches 10N Bridger, 11 inches Billings West End(8 to 11 inches reported on Rimrock Road), 8 inches 2SE Park City (5 to 8 inches with numerous 2-3 foot drifts, significant and numerous drifting across many area roads).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On February 19 and 20, a winter storm event brought heavy snow and gusty winds to portions of Northwest Montana. Poor visibility in blowing snow was reported by Montana DOT.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Total new snowfall amounts ranged from 4 to 7 inches in the valleys up to 11 inches in the mountains. Wind gusts of 25 to 40 mph caused areas of blowing and drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On February 16, a strong winter storm brought very strong gusty west winds of 60 to 100 mph to Powell County in western Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Powell County, strong sustained west winds of 30 mph with gusts of 40 to 80 mph were common from the late evening hours of 2/15 through the overnight hours of 2/16. Highest wind gust occurred at McDonald Pass with a gust to 110 mph at 140 am and another gust to 78 mph at 415 am. Near Avon wind gusts of 50 to 60 mph were recorded between 1035 pm and 1225 am.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On June 5, another frontal boundary moved southeast across the area and produced more strong to severe thunderstorms. Given colder air aloft, large hail reports were also received in addition to high wind reports.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm blew down 30-40 pine trees over an 2-3 acre area near Yaak. The largest trees had a diameter of 18 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A high wind event occurred during the early morning hours of the 2nd along the Northern Rocky Mountain Front. Wind gusts to 60 mph were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust to 60 mph was reported by the Cut Bank ASOS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific cold front moved through Southern Montana. Close behind this front, an arctic front swept across the forecast area bringing bitterly cold temperatures. Snow developed across the area, with the most significant accumulations over the Beartooth/Absaroka Mountains and adjacent foothills, especially on north facing slopes. In addition to the snow, gusty winds combined with very cold temperatures created dangerous wind chills of 10 to 25 degrees below zero at times. The following snow totals were received: 10 inches 5W Red Lodge(Cole Creek Snotel), 11 inches 3SSW Luther(Burnt Mountain Snotel), 9 inches 2S Red Lodge, 6 inches in Laurel, Mc Leod, Nye, and Columbus, 9 inches in Absarokee, 8 inches in Fishtail, Emigrant, and Red Lodge Ski Area and 10 inches 1W Pryor.

The intense upper low over Northern Wyoming and Southeast Montana that resulted in the heavy snow and blizzard conditions during the last three days of the month moved into the western Dakotas during the afternoon of the 30th. As it did, a narrow band of wrap around moisture moved southwest across portions of South Central Montana. This resulted in a very localized area of moderate to heavy snow. The following snow amounts were received: 8 to 11 inches on Rimrock Road in Billings, 8 inches at the Billings airport(new daily record), 8 inches 10N Bridger and 5 to 8 inches with numerous 2-3 foot drifts across many roads 2SE Park City.

A storm system moving through the region caused strong low pressure to form over Alberta. As a result, very strong winds occurred in the favored areas of the Beartooth and Absaroka Mountains. In addition, a strong cold front moved across the forecast area bringing in much cooler temperatures. The mountains experienced the heaviest snowfall late Christmas Eve into Christmas morning as a result of an unstable airmass that allowed for rapid accumulations. Windy conditions also contributed to areas of blowing and drifting snow. The following snow totals were received: 9 inches 10WSW Limestone(Placer Basin Snotel), 6 inches 2S Otter and in Otter and 8 inches 5NE Ridge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On May 10, a strong, negatively tilted upper level short wave trough moved Northeast across the area. Strong surface-based instability and mid-level jet support led to supercell development as the system interracted with a thermal trough that was positioned over the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public phoned in a report of nickel-sized hail. The storm developed on the southeast side of Butte and tracked northeast along the Continental Divide.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On May 10, a strong, negatively tilted upper level short wave trough moved Northeast across the area. Strong surface-based instability and mid-level jet support led to supercell development as the system interracted with a thermal trough that was positioned over the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public reported hail stones that that were between quarter and golfball in size. Damage to a couple of cars was observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On May 10, a strong, negatively tilted upper level short wave trough moved Northeast across the area. Strong surface-based instability and mid-level jet support led to supercell development as the system interracted with a thermal trough that was positioned over the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law Enforcement reported .75 inch hail near Pinesdale. A weather spotter reported .88 inch hail 4 miles southeast of Victor from the same storm a few minutes later.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On evening of June 20, a strong short wave trough and surface front moved across Western Montana. Associated strong to severe thunderstorms produced marginally severe hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weather spotter reported penny sized hail near St. Ignatius.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On June 6 and 7, an unseasonably cold upper level low pressure system moved east across the region producing heavy, wet snowfall in the higher elevations of Southwestern Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The late winter weather system produced snow fall amounts of 5 to 8 inches across the higher elevations of the Bitterroot and Sapphire mountains. Twin Lakes and Saddle Mountain received 8 inches of snow accumulations. Skalkaho Summit received 7 inches, and Black Pine received 5 inches of snow accumulations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On June 6 and 7, an unseasonably cold upper level low pressure system moved east across the region producing heavy, wet snowfall in the higher elevations of Southwestern Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The late winter weather system produced snow fall amounts of 6 to 10 inches across the higher elevations near and along the Continental Divide and over the Rattlesnake Range.  Point Six received 10 inches of snow accumulations, Stuart Mountain received 8 inches, Stinkwater and Pistol Creeks received 7 and 6 inches of snow accumulations, respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On June 6 and 7, an unseasonably cold upper level low pressure system moved east across the region producing heavy, wet snowfall in the higher elevations of Southwestern Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The late winter weather system produced snow fall amounts of 4 to 15 inches across the higher elevations near and along the Continental Divide. The Warm Springs Snotel reported 15 inches of snow accumulations. Georgetown Lake and Barker Lakes reported 8 inches, North Fork of Elk Creek 7 inches, Basin Creek and Peterson Meadows 4 inches of snow accumulations. Heavy snow knocked down several trees in the Anaconda area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On June 28, a strong short wave trough moved across Southwest Montana during the afternoon and evening hours. Isolated severe thunderstorms produced several severe downbursts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several reports of estimated wind gusts to 60 mph from weather spotters and the public were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful Spring upper level low slowly moved across Central Wyoming and Southeast Montana during a 48 hour period . As a result, a heavy, prolonged precipitation event occurred. The precipitation started as rain but quickly changed over to snow. The snow became heavy at times with snowfall rates of one to two inches per hour. In addition to the heavy snow, very strong northwest winds of 25 to 40 mph created blizzard conditions across portions of Southern and Southeastern Montana. Zero to near zero visibilities were common as a result of the combined heavy snow and strong winds. |The following are the many reports that were received from this major Spring blizzard: 13 inches in Biddle (2 day liquid equivalent 1.42 inches), 12 inches 14N Albion(snow total from the afternoon of the 29th through 8am on the 30th), 17 inches in Lame Deer, 24 inches with 3 foot drifts in Lodge Grass, 24 inches 1N Busby with drifts to 3 feet, 36 inches 1N Busby with cattle dying, 7 inches in Forsyth, 10 inches in Colstrip, 12 inches in Volborg(2 day liquid equivalent 1.76 inches), 10 inches 44S Ekalaka, 12 inches in Fort Smith, 6 inches 8NNE Powderville, 8 inches 8SW Biddle, 12 inches in Colstrip, 8 inches in Albion(2 day liquid equivalent 1.92 inches), 8 inches at the  Billings Airport (new daily record snowfall), 8 inches 10N Bridger, 11 inches Billings West End(8 to 11 inches reported on Rimrock Road), 8 inches 2SE Park City (5 to 8 inches with numerous 2-3 foot drifts, significant and numerous drifting across many area roads).

On March 27 and 28, a closed low tracked east across central Idaho and SW Montana. Heavy snowfall amounts were received in higher elevations near Georgetown Lake. Emergency travel only was declared for US Highway 93 due to heavy snowfall heading south toward Lost Trail Pass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow prompted emergency travel only declaration of US HWY 93 south of Missoula over Lost Trail Pass. Snotel sites at Black Pine and Skalkaho Pass received between 8 and 9.5 inches of snow, respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On March 27 and 28, a closed low tracked east across central Idaho and SW Montana. Heavy snowfall amounts were received in higher elevations near Georgetown Lake. Emergency travel only was declared for US Highway 93 due to heavy snowfall heading south toward Lost Trail Pass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 9 and 10 inches of heavy snow was received at the North Fork of Elk Creek and Georgetown Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific cold front moved through Southern Montana. Close behind this front, an arctic front swept across the forecast area bringing bitterly cold temperatures. Snow developed across the area, with the most significant accumulations over the Beartooth/Absaroka Mountains and adjacent foothills, especially on north facing slopes. In addition to the snow, gusty winds combined with very cold temperatures created dangerous wind chills of 10 to 25 degrees below zero at times. The following snow totals were received: 10 inches 5W Red Lodge(Cole Creek Snotel), 11 inches 3SSW Luther(Burnt Mountain Snotel), 9 inches 2S Red Lodge, 6 inches in Laurel, Mc Leod, Nye, and Columbus, 9 inches in Absarokee, 8 inches in Fishtail, Emigrant, and Red Lodge Ski Area and 10 inches 1W Pryor.

During the late afternoon and evening of June 16, 2007, a high precipitation supercell thunderstorm tracked from across northern Montana, just to the north of a warm front.  This was the most devastating hail storm to affect the area since at least 1999, and prompted 22 severe thunderstorm and 6 tornado warnings in the NWS Glasgow county warning area. The storm began early in the day just east of Glacier National Park and gradually moved hundreds of miles eastward through the day.  By 3 pm, the storm crossed into the NWS Glasgow area in Phillips County, where it started to make a turn to the southeast.   The storm traveled for over 250 miles before decreasing in Richland County near the North Dakota border a little after 11 pm.  Hail up to baseball size and wind gusts in excess of 85 mph accompanied the storm.  Torrential heavy rainfall was also experienced, prompting seven flash flood warnings to be issued.  The large hail left a hail swath seen on GOES and Terra MODIS satellites that had a path at least 225 miles long, and up to 12 miles wide. ||The most significant damage from the storm occurred in portions of Valley and McCone Counties.  The towns of Glasgow and Nashua, both in Valley County, took a direct hit from the storm.  In Glasgow, southeast winds gusting to 60 to 65 mph ahead of the storm switched into the northwest with gusts in excess of 70 mph.  Golfball to baseball sized hail fell for several minutes before diminishing to penny sized for another 10-20 minutes.  The majority of the homes and businesses in Glasgow had some sort of damage including:  broken windows, damaged shingles, dented or broken gutters and skirting.  Many vehicles also had significant hail damage.  Just the property damage alone in Glasgow was nearly $8.0 million dollars.  As the storm continued east it hit the town of Nashua and every home and business had some form of damage from the baseball size hail and the damaging winds that occurred with it.   The winds were measured at 89 mph just west of town.  Property damage was estimated to be $3 million dollars. | |In rural areas outside of Glasgow and Nashua there were several cottonwood trees completely uprooted.  Horses and cattle were injured by the hail and wind, and Montana Fish, Wildlife and Parks reported many birds and small animals were killed by the hail.  There were thousands of acres of alfalfa, wheat, and corn that were completely destroyed.  At this time, damage estimates to crops in Valley County are at $20 million dollars.||The storm also produced heavy rainfall of 1 to 2 inches, and was followed later in the evening by additional heavy rain.  Rainfall of 2 to 7 inches fell in the Antelope, Brazil and Willow Creek basins.  The Antelope Creek basin had its most significant flood on record and one road was completely underwater for nearly a third of a mile.  One ranch was isolated by the flood waters for three days. ||As the storm continued across northern McCone County and into western Richland County it continued to produce hail up to the size of ping pong balls and wind gusts that were estimated as high as 80 to 90 mph knocked down telephone and electrical poles.   There was significant damage to farms and ranches in the Vida area, as well as significant loss of crops, estimated at $5 million dollars.  The rural nature of the area minimized the property damage that could have occurred.  ||The overall estimated damage in this storm between the hail and wind damage, and the subsequent flooding is 34.2 million dollars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two to seven inches of rain fell in the Antelope, Brazil and Willow Creek Basins and caused the worst flood on record on the Antelope Creek.  A portion of Billingsly road was inaccessible and completely under water for 3 days.  Flash flooding occurred for approximately 12 hours, but was followed by significant overland flooding for the following few to several days.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front crossed the area and produced widespread severe weather across northeast Montana.  This was the biggest outbreak of severe weather during the month of May during at least the last 5 years.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This observation was from the King Coulee RAWS site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad area of low pressure which extended from the Dakotas south into the southern Rockies brought a period of snow to a portion of northeast Montana during the evening hours of the 28th into the early morning hours of the 29th.  The bulk of the snow fell from eastern Valley County and points east to the North Dakota border.  Snowfall amounts were generally in the 2 to 4 inch range.  A few isolated spots across the Big Sheep Mountains in Dawson and southern McCone Counties had up to 6 inches of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two to four inches of snow fell in extreme eastern Valley County in the vicinity of Lustre.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms occurred during the evening hours of the 24th. Heavy rain caused flash flooding in Richland County where water was flowing over a small stock dam.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain amounting to 1.95 inches fell within 35 minutes and led to flash flooding with water flowing over a small stock dam.  A total of 2.5 inches of rain fell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major severe weather episode occurred from mid-afternoon on the 2nd through the pre-dawn hours of the 3rd.  A very moist and unstable southwest flow aloft was responsible for the severe weather. Although the majority of the reports were of either large hail or strong wind gusts, there were a few spots that had flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two inches of rain fell in less than an hour with a total of over 3 inches of rain in 2 hours.  The heavy rain led to flash flooding in a parking lot.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system moved across Southern Canada. This combined with high pressure over the Great Basin resulted in high winds along the north slopes of the Beartooth Mountains. In addition, a powerful jet stream moved across the Billings Forecast area. The above factors combined with the passage of a strong cold front generated damaging winds across the area.||The gap favored wind areas, such as Judith Gap and Livingston, which are accustomed to strong winds of 60 to 75 mph occuring a couple times a year, experienced wind gusts to 85 mph. This resulted in power being knocked out to the town of Livingston. Winds over 100 mph were recorded at the Stillwater Mine near Nye. In Nye, local news reports quoted local residents indicating wind gusts in excess of 120 mph at times. Extensive damage was done to the area including tree and power pole damage, roofs blown off houses, barns destroyed, and car and home windows being blown out. ||In addition to the direct wind damage caused by this event, wind driven fires possibly sparked by downed power lines engulfed large chunks of land during the evening of the 12th. The fire with the biggest impact was the Chi Chi Fire in Northern Sweet Grass County. The fire grew to 20,000 acres in a few hours on the evening of the 12th. Fire crews on the line reported frequent gusts to 80 mph occurring east of the Crazy Mountains. These gusts were responsible for the rapid spread of the fire which consumed several structures, a bridge, and one fire vehicle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Billings Airport reported 44 mph sustained winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front and upper level disturbance moved across the  central and eastern counties in the Billings County Warning Area during the late afternoon and evening hours of the 13th. Several thunderstorms developed across Northern Stillwater and Sweet Grass Counties before evolving into Supercell thunderstorms over Northern Yellowstone County. These thunderstorms then merged into a squall line across Eastern Yellowstone County and Treasure County before moving across Rosebud, Custer and Fallon Counties. Very large hail and damaging winds accompanied these thunderstorms with widespread damage reported across these areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous power outages were reported across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper level disturbance produced a light snowfall most places, but a few higher amounts especially along north facing slopes.  Some reports include 7 inches at Lewistown Airport, 12 inches at Stringer Creek Snotel, and 15 inches at Crystal Lake Snotel.  Elsewhere, amounts were generally in the 1 to 3 inch range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Seven inches of snow was reported at the Lewistown Aiport and 15 inches at Crystal Lake Snotel.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On February 12, heavy snow fell over Southwest Montana with new snow accumulations of 8 to 10 inches reported from around Butte to Anaconda and Georgetown Lake.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotters from Anaconda reported 9 inches of new snow. Five to six inches of new snow was reported around Butte with up to 10 inches reported near Walkerville. Georgetown Lake received 8 inches new snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On May 10, a strong, negatively tilted upper level short wave trough moved Northeast across the area. Strong surface-based instability and mid-level jet support led to supercell development as the system interracted with a thermal trough that was positioned over the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One inch diameter hail was reported from the Grant Creek area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tree damage and head stones blown over in cemetery in Butte from localized high wind event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ten or more trees blown down on South Montana street in Butte. Head stones in a cemetery south of town were also blown over by strong convective winds. One tree was blown onto a house on Delaware Street. Power was knocked out for four hours at radio and T.V. stations. Gusty erratic winds were caused by passing convective showers falling through a very dry airmass. Bert Mooney Airport reported wind gusts up to 47 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the afternoon of August 5, a weak disturbance moved northeast across Ravalli County. Isolated strong to severe thunderstorms developed as the disturbance moved across the county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm moved across the Burnt Fork area near Stevensville during the evening hours and blew the roof off of a building.

These thunderstorms then merged into a squall line across Eastern Yellowstone County and Treasure County before moving across Rosebud, Custer and Fallon Counties. Very large hail and damaging winds accompanied these thunderstorms with widespread damage reported across these areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: No damage was reported.

On November 12, a strong cold front moved across Western Montana and produced heavy snowfall and high winds in the Bitterroot and Sapphire Mountains as well as high winds in the Anaconda and Deer Lodge areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous wind gusts between 50 and 90 mph were reported in both Anaconda and Deer Lodge. Anaconda was hardest hit. At least 25 structures lost their roofs. Residents reported debris blowing across town. There were two event-related injuries. At least 8 trees were reported to have been blown down by winds. Windows were blown out at the county courthouse. Wind damage to fences and signs were also reported in Anaconda. The Anaconda Emergency Manager reported a least one home per block sustained roof damage. The governor and the county emergency manager declared a State of Emergency for this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On November 18 and 19, a very moist Pacific storm system moved Northeast across Western Montana. The cold and moist airmass moved overtop a dome of colder air. This enhanced precipitation snowfall rates and amounts across the area. In addition to the heavy snowfall, upper level winds mixed to the surface to create breezy conditions in the Bitterroot and Sapphire mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Weather spotters near Evaro Hill reported 12 to 13.5 inches of snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On June 29, a strong, negatively tilted shortwave trough moved to the northeast across the region. An intense mid level jet streak moved around the base of the trough and through western Montana during the early evening hours. A pair of intense storms moved across the area producing widespread damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds blew numerous large trees down onto power lines, buildings, vehicles and roads in and around Yaak. Power was out to the entire community. Several large trees were blown down onto power lines in Libby from straight line winds. From Eureka to Port of Roosville, straight line winds blew numerous large trees and limbs down onto power lines and vehicles. One carport was blown into a field, and roof damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On June 29, a strong, negatively tilted shortwave trough moved to the northeast across the region. A pair of intense storms moved across the area producing widespread damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds were reported to have blown down several trees and large limbs down onto power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On August 5, approaching convective showers brought gusty erratic wind gusts up to 58 mph that caused localized wind damage in Missoula.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong outflow winds from approaching showers caused some minor damage west of Missoula. A power pole snapped in half and landed on the Taco Time Building on North Reserve Street. Northwest Energy reported power failures and some trees knocked down in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure across southern Canada and high pressure over eastern Idaho resulted in tight pressure gradients across Livingston and the Beartooth/Absaroka foothills areas. Very strong winds occurred in Livingston and along the Stillwater River around Nye. The following wind reports were received: 60 mph wind gust in Rapelje, 45 mph sustained winds in Harlowton, 63 mph sustained winds 6NE Livingston, 63 mph sustained winds 7NE Livingston, 79 mph wind gust 7NE Livingston and a 76 mph wind gust in Nye.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific weather system brought gusty winds to North Central Montana. Wind gusts in excess of 80 mph were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A gust to 58 mph was reported at Moccasin. The strong winds contributed to four separate accidents involving semi-trailer trucks on Highway 87 between Mile Markers 29 and 40. Additionally, power was knocked out in parts of Geyser, Hobson and Windham.

A snow storm produced widely varying snowfall amounts across northeast Montana.  Amounts ranged from less than an inch to over a foot.  The most snow was reported in southeast Petroleum and southwest Garfield Counties, where amounts ranged from 9 to 13 inches.  The area from northeast Roosevelt County into southern Sheridan County received from 7 to 9 inches of snow.  4 to 7 inch amounts were common from eastern Daniels County and northern Sheridan County as well as a large area from southeast Garfield County through southern McCone County, and from Central Dawson County into northern Wibaux and Richland Counties.  Less than an inch of snow was observed in Western Valley and Phillips Counties.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong westerly flow aloft along with deep low pressure over central Canada combined to produce strong and gusty winds over a portion of North Central Montana. Wind gusts in excess of 60 mph were common. Wind gusts along the Northern Rocky Mountain Front were mainly 80 to |100 mph, with the highest gust of 110 mph measured at Heart Butte. Property damages, estimated to be $350,000, occurred on the Blackfeet Indian Reservation.

Water was flowing over Rock Springs Road where the Otter Creek empties into the Big Muddy Creek approximately 1 mile southwest of Reserve.   The water covered about 100-150 yards of road.  The Big Muddy was a bit below flood stage at the time, but had enough water in it that it caused the Otter Creek to back up onto the road.

On July 18, an intensely strong shortwave trough moved to the northeast across Western Montana during the late afternoon and evening hours. The breaking of a strong mid level cap released copious amount of low level instability which aided in the rapid development of severe thunderstorm super cells.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: National Weather Service storm survey crew saw numerous trees and several power poles down along the storm's track. Two structures received roof damage. The damage observed was indicative of straight line winds. A flag pole was bent in half southwest of Polson. Based upon the damage observed, winds were estimated to be between 80 to 100 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On July 18, an intensely strong shortwave trough moved to the northeast across Western Montana during the late afternoon and evening hours. The breaking of a strong mid level cap released copious amount of low level instability which aided in the rapid development of severe thunderstorm super cells.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees and power poles were blown down along the northern shores of Flathead lake. The roof to Carpenter's Arena near Kalispell was blown off. Damage to two other roofs was observed. One small grain silo was blown over near Somers. The damage observed from the NWS storm survey crew was indicative of straight line wind damage. Based upon the damage observed, winds were estimated to be between 80 and 100 mph.

The breaking of a strong mid level cap released copious amount of low level instability which aided in the rapid development of severe thunderstorm super cells.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estimated thunderstorm wind gusts to 70 mph produced substantial structural damage to the drive-in theater and the town ball park. Numerous large trees were also blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On July 17, a strong short wave trough moved to the northeast across West Central Montana during the evening hours. Isolated severe thunderstorms were able to mix down upper level winds to create isolated wind damage in both Lake and Sanders Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorm winds blew down a large tree and several large branches in Ravalli.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter report from Dixon School of fences, power poles and large spruce trees with 2 1/2 diameter trunks broken and blown over.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golf ball size hail was reported to KECI TV Meteorologist from a weather spotter in Tarkio.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad area of low pressure which extended from the Dakotas south into the southern Rockies brought a period of snow to a portion of northeast Montana during the evening hours of the 28th into the early morning hours of the 29th.  The bulk of the snow fell from eastern Valley County and points east to the North Dakota border.  Snowfall amounts were generally in the 2 to 4 inch range.  A few isolated spots across the Big Sheep Mountains in Dawson and southern McCone Counties had up to 6 inches of snow.
On December 19 and 20, a strong disturbance moved east across Western Montana. Snowfall amounts were enhanced by a strong and moist westerly flow that was already entrenched over the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lolo Pass received 13 inches and Lost Trail Pass 8 inches of new snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On December 23 and 24, another strong disturbance moved east across Western Montana and brought heavy snowfall accumulations to the high elevations and the Seeley-Swan valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Highway 83 corridor between Seeley Lake and the Swan River Lodge received between 6 and 10 inches of new snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On December 23 and 24, another strong disturbance moved east across Western Montana and brought heavy snowfall accumulations to the high elevations and the Seeley-Swan valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lookout Pass Ski Resort received 9 inches and Mullan Pass received 8.5 inches of new snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On December 29 and 30, moist northwesterly flow persisted over the northern Rockies. During this period, a series of shortwave disturbances moved through and produced heavy snow accumulations and strong winds across Western Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway 2 corridor between Hungry Horse and Marias Pass received 8.5 to 18 inches of new snowfall. In addition, winds to 20 mph were reported along this route from the Fielding RAWS weather station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On December 2 and 3, a potent winter storm moved across Western Montana. Heavy snowfall and gusty winds were observed at many mountain locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Troy received a record 30 to 36 inches of snowfall during the event. Yaak received 21 to 28 inches of snowfall from the event.  Trained snowfall reporters in Noxon and at Bull Lake reported 9.7 inches and 8 inches of new snowfall, respectively. In addition to heavy snowfall, wind gusts to 32 miles per hour were reported at the Eureka airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On December 2 and 3, a potent winter storm moved across Western Montana. Heavy snowfall and gusty winds were observed at many mountain locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lookout Pass received 33 inches of new snowfall accumulations from this storm. Thompson Falls received 11.0 inches, and Heron received 16.4 inches. In addition to the very heavy snowfall, Lookout Pass had wind gusts as high as 52 mph. Nine mile reported wind gusts as high as 30 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On December 2 and 3, a potent winter storm moved across Western Montana. Heavy snowfall and gusty winds were observed at many mountain locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lolo Pass and Twelve Mile Creek both received 20 inches of new snowfall accumulations from this storm. Nez Perce Camp received 8.0 inches. In addition to the very heavy snowfall, most mountain weather observation sites reported wind gusts between 29 and 43 mph during this period.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Automated weather observation station at Seeley Lake reported 16 inches of new snowfall. A snowfall reporter in Condon reported 8 inches of new snow. In addition, weather observation stations in Greenough and Lower Rock Creek reported gusty winds to 21 and 23 mph respectively.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ten inches of new snowfall accumulations were reported by MDOT near Essex at 7:00 pm. Twelve to fourteen inches of new snowfall accumulations were reported by weather observation sites between West Glacier and Essex by the end of the event. In addition, blowing and drifting snow was also observed in these areas.

A narrow band of heavy snowfall affected an area from Bainville southwest through Terry, and points east to the North Dakota border. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unseasonably warm temperatures caused high runoff levels from snowmelt off the Bridger Mountains, and flooding of poor drainage areas in and around Bozeman.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Unseasonably warm temperatures produced unexpectedly high levels of runoff of many streams from melting snow from the Bridger Mountains. Ten to eighteen inches of water was reported in the backyards of several homes. Reports of many intersections in the city of Bozeman  almost completely underwater were common. Many irrigation ditches across the county were overflowing and running across roadways. No serious damages were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Additional heavy rainfall after a record wet May caused the Antelope Creek in southern Valley County to come out of it's banks during the early morning hours of the 1st.  The creek was overrunning portions of Billingsley Road.  The Tallow Creek in southern Phillips County also came out of it's banks and washed out a gravel road.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Antelope Creek was out of it's banks and overrunning portions of Billingsley Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated severe thunderstorms in northern Phillips County produced high winds during the late evening.

The month of May produced record rainfall in spots across northeast Montana.  Heavy rainfall from the 20th to the 23rd led to some small stream flooding in parts of Valley and Phillips County from the evening of the 22nd through the early afternoon of the 25th.  Tallow Creek in southeast Phillips County was out of its banks in several locations.  Both Timber and Square Creek in southwest Valley County were out of their banks or nearly so.  Low lying areas near Brazil Creek (just west of Glasgow) had significant ponding of water with some outbuildings affected.  There were no reports of any major flooding or structures that were damaged.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Timber and Square Creek were out of their banks or nearly so. Low lying areas near Brazil Creek (just west of Glasgow) had significant ponding of water with some outbuildings affected.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated severe thunderstorms produced nickel hail in Flatwillow and 60 mph wind gusts 8 miles east of Loring.
Isolated severe thunderstorms produced nickel hail in Flatwillow and 60 mph wind gusts 8 miles east of Loring.

Thunderstorms moved through areas just east of Molt during the evening of March 27th. Wind damage occurred 5 miles east of Molt at approximately 610 pm. A mobile home trailer was tossed over and destroyed. In addition, a porch roof was blown off of a house and a small shed was flipped over. The National Weather Service concluded that this wind damage was caused by 70 to 80 mph microburst winds. A microburst is a strong outflow of wind from thunderstorms that can creat damage similar to a weak tornado.

An isolated microburst destroyed a pig shed 8 miles south of Terry. Other buildings on the property were undamaged.  The strongest wind gust reported was 31 miles to the southwest in Miles City with a gust to 48 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm was dying and the outflow winds from it damaged a 14' by 44' hog shed and pushed it 75 feet from its slab.  The nearest measured wind was at the Miles City Airport with a gust of 48 mph.  Other buildings in the area were not damaged at all and no other reports of severe weather occurred in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the late afternoon and evening of June 16, 2007, a high precipitation supercell thunderstorm tracked from across northern Montana, just to the north of a warm front.  This was the most devastating hail storm to affect the area since at least 1999, and prompted 22 severe thunderstorm and 6 tornado warnings in the NWS Glasgow county warning area. The storm began early in the day just east of Glacier National Park and gradually moved hundreds of miles eastward through the day.  By 3 pm, the storm crossed into the NWS Glasgow area in Phillips County, where it started to make a turn to the southeast.   The storm traveled for over 250 miles before decreasing in Richland County near the North Dakota border a little after 11 pm.  Hail up to baseball size and wind gusts in excess of 85 mph accompanied the storm.  Torrential heavy rainfall was also experienced, prompting seven flash flood warnings to be issued.  The large hail left a hail swath seen on GOES and Terra MODIS satellites that had a path at least 225 miles long, and up to 12 miles wide. ||The most significant damage from the storm occurred in portions of Valley and McCone Counties.  The towns of Glasgow and Nashua, both in Valley County, took a direct hit from the storm.  In Glasgow, southeast winds gusting to 60 to 65 mph ahead of the storm switched into the northwest with gusts in excess of 70 mph.  Golfball to baseball sized hail fell for several minutes before diminishing to penny sized for another 10-20 minutes.  The majority of the homes and businesses in Glasgow had some sort of damage including:  broken windows, damaged shingles, dented or broken gutters and skirting.  Many vehicles also had significant hail damage.  Just the property damage alone in Glasgow was nearly $8.0 million dollars.  As the storm continued east it hit the town of Nashua and every home and business had some form of damage from the baseball size hail and the damaging winds that occurred with it.   The winds were measured at 89 mph just west of town.  Property damage was estimated to be $3 million dollars. | |In rural areas outside of Glasgow and Nashua there were several cottonwood trees completely uprooted.  Horses and cattle were injured by the hail and wind, and Montana Fish, Wildlife and Parks reported many birds and small animals were killed by the hail.  There were thousands of acres of alfalfa, wheat, and corn that were completely destroyed.  At this time, damage estimates to crops in Valley County are at $20 million dollars.||The storm also produced heavy rainfall of 1 to 2 inches, and was followed later in the evening by additional heavy rain.  Rainfall of 2 to 7 inches fell in the Antelope, Brazil and Willow Creek basins.  The Antelope Creek basin had its most significant flood on record and one road was completely underwater for nearly a third of a mile.  One ranch was isolated by the flood waters for three days. ||As the storm continued across northern McCone County and into western Richland County it continued to produce hail up to the size of ping pong balls and wind gusts that were estimated as high as 80 to 90 mph knocked down telephone and electrical poles.   There was significant damage to farms and ranches in the Vida area, as well as significant loss of crops, estimated at $5 million dollars.  The rural nature of the area minimized the property damage that could have occurred.  ||The overall estimated damage in this storm between the hail and wind damage, and the subsequent flooding is 34.2 million dollars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: See entire episode narrative for full details.

Golfball to baseball sized hail fell for several minutes before diminishing to penny sized for another 10-20 minutes.  The majority of the homes and businesses in Glasgow had some sort of damage including:  broken windows, damaged shingles, dented or broken gutters and skirting.  Many vehicles also had significant hail damage.  Just the property damage alone in Glasgow was nearly $8.0 million dollars.  As the storm continued east it hit the town of Nashua and every home and business had some form of damage from the baseball size hail and the damaging winds that occurred with it.   The winds were measured at 89 mph just west of town.  Property damage was estimated to be $3 million dollars. | |In rural areas outside of Glasgow and Nashua there were several cottonwood trees completely uprooted.  Horses and cattle were injured by the hail and wind, and Montana Fish, Wildlife and Parks reported many birds and small animals were killed by the hail.  There were thousands of acres of alfalfa, wheat, and corn that were completely destroyed.  At this time, damage estimates to crops in Valley County are at $20 million dollars.||The storm also produced heavy rainfall of 1 to 2 inches, and was followed later in the evening by additional heavy rain.  Rainfall of 2 to 7 inches fell in the Antelope, Brazil and Willow Creek basins.  The Antelope Creek basin had its most significant flood on record and one road was completely underwater for nearly a third of a mile.  One ranch was isolated by the flood waters for three days. ||As the storm continued across northern McCone County and into western Richland County it continued to produce hail up to the size of ping pong balls and wind gusts that were estimated as high as 80 to 90 mph knocked down telephone and electrical poles.   There was significant damage to farms and ranches in the Vida area, as well as significant loss of crops, estimated at $5 million dollars.  The rural nature of the area minimized the property damage that could have occurred.  ||The overall estimated damage in this storm between the hail and wind damage, and the subsequent flooding is 34.2 million dollars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: See entire episode narrative for full details.

This was the most devastating hail storm to affect the area since at least 1999, and prompted 22 severe thunderstorm and 6 tornado warnings in the NWS Glasgow county warning area. The storm began early in the day just east of Glacier National Park and gradually moved hundreds of miles eastward through the day.  By 3 pm, the storm crossed into the NWS Glasgow area in Phillips County, where it started to make a turn to the southeast.   The storm traveled for over 250 miles before decreasing in Richland County near the North Dakota border a little after 11 pm.  Hail up to baseball size and wind gusts in excess of 85 mph accompanied the storm.  Torrential heavy rainfall was also experienced, prompting seven flash flood warnings to be issued.  The large hail left a hail swath seen on GOES and Terra MODIS satellites that had a path at least 225 miles long, and up to 12 miles wide. ||The most significant damage from the storm occurred in portions of Valley and McCone Counties.  The towns of Glasgow and Nashua, both in Valley County, took a direct hit from the storm.  In Glasgow, southeast winds gusting to 60 to 65 mph ahead of the storm switched into the northwest with gusts in excess of 70 mph.  Golfball to baseball sized hail fell for several minutes before diminishing to penny sized for another 10-20 minutes.  The majority of the homes and businesses in Glasgow had some sort of damage including:  broken windows, damaged shingles, dented or broken gutters and skirting.  Many vehicles also had significant hail damage.  Just the property damage alone in Glasgow was nearly $8.0 million dollars.  As the storm continued east it hit the town of Nashua and every home and business had some form of damage from the baseball size hail and the damaging winds that occurred with it.   The winds were measured at 89 mph just west of town.  Property damage was estimated to be $3 million dollars. | |In rural areas outside of Glasgow and Nashua there were several cottonwood trees completely uprooted.  Horses and cattle were injured by the hail and wind, and Montana Fish, Wildlife and Parks reported many birds and small animals were killed by the hail. The Antelope Creek basin had its most significant flood on record and one road was completely underwater for nearly a third of a mile.  One ranch was isolated by the flood waters for three days. ||As the storm continued across northern McCone County and into western Richland County it continued to produce hail up to the size of ping pong balls and wind gusts that were estimated as high as 80 to 90 mph knocked down telephone and electrical poles.   There was significant damage to farms and ranches in the Vida area, as well as significant loss of crops, estimated at $5 million dollars.  The rural nature of the area minimized the property damage that could have occurred.  ||The overall estimated damage in this storm between the hail and wind damage, and the subsequent flooding is 34.2 million dollars.

The storm began early in the day just east of Glacier National Park and gradually moved hundreds of miles eastward through the day.  By 3 pm, the storm crossed into the NWS Glasgow area in Phillips County, where it started to make a turn to the southeast.   The storm traveled for over 250 miles before decreasing in Richland County near the North Dakota border a little after 11 pm.  Hail up to baseball size and wind gusts in excess of 85 mph accompanied the storm.  Torrential heavy rainfall was also experienced, prompting seven flash flood warnings to be issued.  The large hail left a hail swath seen on GOES and Terra MODIS satellites that had a path at least 225 miles long, and up to 12 miles wide. ||The most significant damage from the storm occurred in portions of Valley and McCone Counties.  The towns of Glasgow and Nashua, both in Valley County, took a direct hit from the storm.  In Glasgow, southeast winds gusting to 60 to 65 mph ahead of the storm switched into the northwest with gusts in excess of 70 mph.  Golfball to baseball sized hail fell for several minutes before diminishing to penny sized for another 10-20 minutes.  The majority of the homes and businesses in Glasgow had some sort of damage including:  broken windows, damaged shingles, dented or broken gutters and skirting.  Many vehicles also had significant hail damage.  Just the property damage alone in Glasgow was nearly $8.0 million dollars.  As the storm continued east it hit the town of Nashua and every home and business had some form of damage from the baseball size hail and the damaging winds that occurred with it.   The winds were measured at 89 mph just west of town.  Property damage was estimated to be $3 million dollars. | |In rural areas outside of Glasgow and Nashua there were several cottonwood trees completely uprooted.  Horses and cattle were injured by the hail and wind, and Montana Fish, Wildlife and Parks reported many birds and small animals were killed by the hail.  There were thousands of acres of alfalfa, wheat, and corn that were completely destroyed.  At this time, damage estimates to crops in Valley County are at $20 million dollars.||The storm also produced heavy rainfall of 1 to 2 inches, and was followed later in the evening by additional heavy rain.  Rainfall of 2 to 7 inches fell in the Antelope, Brazil and Willow Creek basins.  The Antelope Creek basin had its most significant flood on record and one road was completely underwater for nearly a third of a mile.  One ranch was isolated by the flood waters for three days. ||As the storm continued across northern McCone County and into western Richland County it continued to produce hail up to the size of ping pong balls and wind gusts that were estimated as high as 80 to 90 mph knocked down telephone and electrical poles.   There was significant damage to farms and ranches in the Vida area, as well as significant loss of crops, estimated at $5 million dollars.  The rural nature of the area minimized the property damage that could have occurred.  ||The overall estimated damage in this storm between the hail and wind damage, and the subsequent flooding is 34.2 million dollars.

There was significant damage to farms and ranches in the Vida area, as well as significant loss of crops, estimated at $5 million dollars.  The rural nature of the area minimized the property damage that could have occurred.  ||The overall estimated damage in this storm between the hail and wind damage, and the subsequent flooding is 34.2 million dollars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was an unknown amount of crop damage.

At this time, damage estimates to crops in Valley County are at $20 million dollars.||The storm also produced heavy rainfall of 1 to 2 inches, and was followed later in the evening by additional heavy rain.  Rainfall of 2 to 7 inches fell in the Antelope, Brazil and Willow Creek basins.  The Antelope Creek basin had its most significant flood on record and one road was completely underwater for nearly a third of a mile.  One ranch was isolated by the flood waters for three days. ||As the storm continued across northern McCone County and into western Richland County it continued to produce hail up to the size of ping pong balls and wind gusts that were estimated as high as 80 to 90 mph knocked down telephone and electrical poles.   There was significant damage to farms and ranches in the Vida area, as well as significant loss of crops, estimated at $5 million dollars.  The rural nature of the area minimized the property damage that could have occurred.  ||The overall estimated damage in this storm between the hail and wind damage, and the subsequent flooding is 34.2 million dollars.

During the late afternoon and evening of June 16, 2007, a high precipitation supercell thunderstorm tracked from across northern Montana, just to the north of a warm front.  This was the most devastating hail storm to affect the area since at least 1999, and prompted 22 severe thunderstorm and 6 tornado warnings in the NWS Glasgow county warning area. The storm began early in the day just east of Glacier National Park and gradually moved hundreds of miles eastward through the day.  By 3 pm, the storm crossed into the NWS Glasgow area in Phillips County, where it started to make a turn to the southeast.   The storm traveled for over 250 miles before decreasing in Richland County near the North Dakota border a little after 11 pm.  Hail up to baseball size and wind gusts in excess of 85 mph accompanied the storm.  Torrential heavy rainfall was also experienced, prompting seven flash flood warnings to be issued.  The large hail left a hail swath seen on GOES and Terra MODIS satellites that had a path at least 225 miles long, and up to 12 miles wide. ||The most significant damage from the storm occurred in portions of Valley and McCone Counties.  The towns of Glasgow and Nashua, both in Valley County, took a direct hit from the storm.  In Glasgow, southeast winds gusting to 60 to 65 mph ahead of the storm switched into the northwest with gusts in excess of 70 mph.  Golfball to baseball sized hail fell for several minutes before diminishing to penny sized for another 10-20 minutes.  The majority of the homes and businesses in Glasgow had some sort of damage including:  broken windows, damaged shingles, dented or broken gutters and skirting.  Many vehicles also had significant hail damage.  Just the property damage alone in Glasgow was nearly $8.0 million dollars.  As the storm continued east it hit the town of Nashua and every home and business had some form of damage from the baseball size hail and the damaging winds that occurred with it.   The winds were measured at 89 mph just west of town.  Property damage was estimated to be $3 million dollars. | |In rural areas outside of Glasgow and Nashua there were several cottonwood trees completely uprooted.  Horses and cattle were injured by the hail and wind, and Montana Fish, Wildlife and Parks reported many birds and small animals were killed by the hail.  There were thousands of acres of alfalfa, wheat, and corn that were completely destroyed.  At this time, damage estimates to crops in Valley County are at $20 million dollars.||The storm also produced heavy rainfall of 1 to 2 inches, and was followed later in the evening by additional heavy rain.  Rainfall of 2 to 7 inches fell in the Antelope, Brazil and Willow Creek basins.  The Antelope Creek basin had its most significant flood on record and one road was completely underwater for nearly a third of a mile.  One ranch was isolated by the flood waters for three days. ||As the storm continued across northern McCone County and into western Richland County it continued to produce hail up to the size of ping pong balls and wind gusts that were estimated as high as 80 to 90 mph knocked down telephone and electrical poles.   There was significant damage to farms and ranches in the Vida area, as well as significant loss of crops, estimated at $5 million dollars.  The rural nature of the area minimized the property damage that could have occurred.  ||The overall estimated damage in this storm between the hail and wind damage, and the subsequent flooding is 34.2 million dollars.
