A strong Pacific jet stream moved over the Billings Forecast Area beginning the afternoon of the 2nd and continued through the morning of the 4th. As a result, conditions became favorable for strong winds at the surface. The winds were the strongest during the overnight hours of the 2nd and 3rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fisher Creek Snotel measured 8 inches of snow in 12 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system moved across Southern Canada. This combined with high pressure over the Great Basin resulted in high winds along the north slopes of the Beartooth Mountains. In addition, a powerful jet stream moved across the Billings Forecast area. The above factors combined with the passage of a strong cold front generated damaging winds across the area.||The gap favored wind areas, such as Judith Gap and Livingston, which are accustomed to strong winds of 60 to 75 mph occuring a couple times a year, experienced wind gusts to 85 mph. This resulted in power being knocked out to the town of Livingston. Winds over 100 mph were recorded at the Stillwater Mine near Nye. In Nye, local news reports quoted local residents indicating wind gusts in excess of 120 mph at times. Extensive damage was done to the area including tree and power pole damage, roofs blown off houses, barns destroyed, and car and home windows being blown out. ||In addition to the direct wind damage caused by this event, wind driven fires possibly sparked by downed power lines engulfed large chunks of land during the evening of the 12th. The fire with the biggest impact was the Chi Chi Fire in Northern Sweet Grass County. The fire grew to 20,000 acres in a few hours on the evening of the 12th. Fire crews on the line reported frequent gusts to 80 mph occurring east of the Crazy Mountains. These gusts were responsible for the rapid spread of the fire which consumed several structures, a bridge, and one fire vehicle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 50x100ft pole barn was disintigrated with debris scattered everywhere due to 100+ mph wind gusts along Fiddler Creek Road near Nye. Wind gusts were recorded at 100 mph at 957 pm at the Stillwater Mine.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous upper level storm system and strong cold front produced a widespread severe weather outbreak in northeastern Montana.  The majority of the events were high wind, a few reports of large hail, and two tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several outbuildings on the property were leaning to the east, and shingles were found at least 100 yards away.  A refrigerator was flipped off the front porch of a house, and water was sucked up out of a four foot tall pool in the back yard.  A windmill was also twisted and a steer barn collapsed.  Based on the NWS damage survey the damage was estimated as EF1 where the steer barn collapsed, and the remainder of the damage was estimated at EF0.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous upper level storm system and strong cold front produced a widespread severe weather outbreak in northeastern Montana.  The majority of the events were high wind, a few reports of large hail, and two tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A fully loaded grain truck weighing 40,000 to 45,000 pounds was in the path of this tornado.  A bolted down upper portion of the truck's grain bin, which weighed 400 pounds, was lifted straight into the air causing minimal damage to the rest of the truck bin.  It then spun overhead and slammed into the ground twice at a distance of about 80 feet from the vehicle.  A witness reported a rotating wall cloud and a rain wrapped tornado.  A damage survey was conducted by the NWS and the tornado was rated in the low EF1 range.

A storm system moving through the region caused strong low pressure to form over Alberta. As a result, very strong winds occurred in the favored areas of the Beartooth and Absaroka Mountains. In addition, a strong cold front moved across the forecast area bringing in much cooler temperatures. The mountains experienced the heaviest snowfall late Christmas Eve into Christmas morning as a result of an unstable airmass that allowed for rapid accumulations. Windy conditions also contributed to areas of blowing and drifting snow. The following snow totals were received: 9 inches 10WSW Limestone(Placer Basin Snotel), 6 inches 2S Otter and in Otter and 8 inches 5NE Ridge.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: East Livingston DOT measured 62 mph sustained winds with a gust of 78 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist westerly flow aloft combined with cold low level air to generate heavy snow across portions of Southern Montana and North Central Wyoming. In addition, a weather disturbance moved across the area helping to create periods of intense snowfall. The following snow totals were received: 7 inches in Livingston; 13 inches in Nye; 10 inches 11 ESE Chico(Placer Basin Snotel), and in Big Timber; 12 inches 2N Red Lodge and Judith Gap; 8 inches 4NE Martinsdale(South Fork Shields Snotel)and in Pray; 9 inches 4NE Martinsdale(Porcupine Snotel), 8 ENE Wilsall and 11NW Silver Gate; 7 inches in Harlowton; 13 inches 4E Roscoe, and 16 inches 3S Washoe(Cole Creek Snotel) and 4SSW Red Lodge(Burnt Mountain Snotel).

Severe thunderstorms developed over portions of Southwest Montana during the late afternoon hours of the 18th. Hail the size of half dollars as well as winds in excess of 60 mph was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm dropped .88 inch diameter hail at Virginia City 2W. The hail damaged 30 windows in Nevada City and caused minor injuries to passengers traveling in an open car train between Nevada City and Virginia City. Wind gusts were reported between 50 and 60 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced a wind gust to 60 mph at Virginia City 2W. The strong wind blew a sign (2 feet by 2 feet) through a windshield of a car, injuring the driver.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated severe weather occurred during the afternoon and evening hours across a portion of northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A woman was caught outside and received several bruises.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated severe weather occurred during the afternoon and evening hours across a portion of northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail covered the ground and was accompanied by heavy rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Outflow boundaries from thunderstorms produced some damaging wind gusts in Phillips and Valley Counties during the evening and overnight hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Outflow from thunderstorms 10 to 15 miles south produced wind damage.  The strong wind lasted about 30 to 40 seconds.  A camper was blown approximately 30 yards and destroyed.  Branches were twisted off of trees and power lines were downed by the wind.  The roofs were blown off of two barns.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major severe weather episode occurred from mid-afternoon on the 2nd through the pre-dawn hours of the 3rd.  A very moist and unstable southwest flow aloft was responsible for the severe weather. Although the majority of the reports were of either large hail or strong wind gusts, there were a few spots that had flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail caused the window in a home to crack.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Outflow boundaries from thunderstorms produced some damaging wind gusts in Phillips and Valley Counties during the evening and overnight hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A shed roof was blown off a distance of 100 yards, and the north door of the shed was blown off.  There were numerous tree limbs down.  A cottonwood limb was ignited by lightning, but did not start a fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front crossed the area and produced severe thunderstorms during the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail ranged in size from pea to nickel.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front crossed the area and produced severe thunderstorms during the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The wind blew down 6 power poles between Terry and Fallon.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm dropped .88 inch diameter hail at Dillon 3N.

A moist westerly flow aloft combined with cold low level air to generate heavy snow across portions of Southern Montana and North Central Wyoming. In addition, a weather disturbance moved across the area helping to create periods of intense snowfall. The following snow totals were received: 7 inches in Livingston; 13 inches in Nye; 10 inches 11 ESE Chico(Placer Basin Snotel), and in Big Timber; 12 inches 2N Red Lodge and Judith Gap; 8 inches 4NE Martinsdale(South Fork Shields Snotel)and in Pray; 9 inches 4NE Martinsdale(Porcupine Snotel), 8 ENE Wilsall and 11NW Silver Gate; 7 inches in Harlowton; 13 inches 4E Roscoe, and 16 inches 3S Washoe(Cole Creek Snotel) and 4SSW Red Lodge(Burnt Mountain Snotel).

A strong storm system moved across Southern Canada. This combined with high pressure over the Great Basin resulted in high winds along the north slopes of the Beartooth Mountains. In addition, a powerful jet stream moved across the Billings Forecast area. The above factors combined with the passage of a strong cold front generated damaging winds across the area.||The gap favored wind areas, such as Judith Gap and Livingston, which are accustomed to strong winds of 60 to 75 mph occuring a couple times a year, experienced wind gusts to 85 mph. This resulted in power being knocked out to the town of Livingston. Winds over 100 mph were recorded at the Stillwater Mine near Nye. In Nye, local news reports quoted local residents indicating wind gusts in excess of 120 mph at times. Extensive damage was done to the area including tree and power pole damage, roofs blown off houses, barns destroyed, and car and home windows being blown out. ||In addition to the direct wind damage caused by this event, wind driven fires possibly sparked by downed power lines engulfed large chunks of land during the evening of the 12th. The fire with the biggest impact was the Chi Chi Fire in Northern Sweet Grass County. The fire grew to 20,000 acres in a few hours on the evening of the 12th. Fire crews on the line reported frequent gusts to 80 mph occurring east of the Crazy Mountains. These gusts were responsible for the rapid spread of the fire which consumed several structures, a bridge, and one fire vehicle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hillsboro RAWS site located 22 miles southwest of Fort Smith reported a 67 mph wind gust. Winds gusted between 58 mph and 67 mph between 340 am MST and 540 am MST.

A strong Pacific jet stream moved over the Billings Forecast Area beginning the afternoon of the 2nd and continued through the morning of the 4th. As a result, conditions became favorable for strong winds at the surface. The winds were the strongest during the overnight hours of the 2nd and 3rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds of 50 mph were recorded at the Livingston ASOS. In addition, 70 mph sustained winds with an 80 mph wind gust was recorded at the East Livingston DOT site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific jet stream moved over the Billings Forecast Area beginning the afternoon of the 2nd and continued through the morning of the 4th. As a result, conditions became favorable for strong winds at the surface. The winds were the strongest during the overnight hours of the 2nd and 3rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Judith Gap recorded 47 mph sustained winds. Several hours of 40 mph sustained winds were also reported at the DOT site.

July brought record heat to much of northeast Montana.  In Glasgow it was the 2nd hottest July and calendar month on record.  Records at Glasgow date back to 1893.  There were a total of 6 days with a high temperature of 100 degrees or higher.  The high of 106F on the 24th was not a record for the date, but was the hottest temperature observed in Glasgow since 1988.  Seven nights had a low temperature of 70F or above, which was the most ever observed in a month.  There were also 5 consecutive nights with a low temperature of 70F or above, which was also a new record.  Record warm lows were set or tied on 5 dates during the month.  Most impressive was the low of 75F on the 21st as this was the warmest low temperature observed in Glasgow since 1983, and also tied with July 8, 1983 for the warmest low temperature ever observed during the month of July.||Even areas along the Canadian border that typically only experience 100 degree temperatures once every 2 to 3 years had a few to several days with high temperatures above 100 degrees. Opheim 10N which only averages a 100 degree day once every 5 years, and had not had a 100 degree day since 1988, had three.  The low temperatures were also unusually warm.  In Glendive where records date back to 1893 the low of 79 degrees on the 25th was the warmest low temperature since August 27, 1931.   Sidney where records date back to 1910 had the warmest low temperature ever observed on the 25th with a low of 78 degrees.  The high temperature of 107 degrees on the 24th and 25th were the hottest temperatures observed in Sidney since July of 1917 when the all-time high temperature of 110 degrees was established.||The hot and mainly dry weather during the second half of the month of July produced moderate agricultural drought conditions by months end.

There were also 5 consecutive nights with a low temperature of 70F or above, which was also a new record.  Record warm lows were set or tied on 5 dates during the month.  Most impressive was the low of 75F on the 21st as this was the warmest low temperature observed in Glasgow since 1983, and also tied with July 8, 1983 for the warmest low temperature ever observed during the month of July.||Even areas along the Canadian border that typically only experience 100 degree temperatures once every 2 to 3 years had a few to several days with high temperatures above 100 degrees. Opheim 10N which only averages a 100 degree day once every 5 years, and had not had a 100 degree day since 1988, had three.  The low temperatures were also unusually warm.  In Glendive where records date back to 1893 the low of 79 degrees on the 25th was the warmest low temperature since August 27, 1931.   Sidney where records date back to 1910 had the warmest low temperature ever observed on the 25th with a low of 78 degrees.  The high temperature of 107 degrees on the 24th and 25th were the hottest temperatures observed in Sidney since July of 1917 when the all-time high temperature of 110 degrees was established.||The hot and mainly dry weather during the second half of the month of July produced moderate agricultural drought conditions by months end.

A large spring storm associated with a cutoff upper level low, moved across the Northern Rockies on the afternoon of June 6th through the morning of June7th. Thunderstorms developed by early afternoon across northern Wyoming and southern Montana, with heavy rainfall beginning in Sheridan, Wyoming and Carbon County, Montana at 330pm and across much of southern Montana by 6pm. By 9pm Wednesday evening, heavy rainfall became more widespread across southern Montana and continued thruogh the overnight hours. By mid morning on the 6th, rainfall had ended across the impacted area. Runoff from areas upstream of Rosebud County and the Tongue River Reservoir resulted in flooding downstream of the dam.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lame Deer was under 3 to 4 feet of water, with homes flooded, people stranded, and a propane tank floating in the street.

A very unstable airmass supported the development of widespread thunderstorms over North Central Montana during the afternoon and evening hours of the 16th and continuing into the early morning hours of the 17th. A tornado touched down in the mountains east of the town of Neihart. Damage was limited to numerous downed trees. Hail the sixe of baseballs was reported at several locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm survey indicated tornadic damage path beginning on the eastern slope of Big Baldy Mountain...then continuing eastward for 5 miles. Path width varied from 10 yards to 100 yards. Damage to trees generally blown down or snapped off. Damage greatest on south side of path with trees converging into the path. Main damage swath continued eastward over an adjacent ridge and down the other side. Near end of damage path...tree fall indicated cyclonic swirl pattern. Tornado damage rated as EF1 consistent with winds of 100 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous upper level storm system and strong cold front produced a widespread severe weather outbreak in northeastern Montana.  The majority of the events were high wind, a few reports of large hail, and two tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The wind caused an air conditioner to be knocked out of a window.  A tree branch broke and caused a window to be broken.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike at approximately 415pm in the Briarwood Subdivision, located 1 mile southeast of Billings, resulted in a wildfire that lasted several days. The main threat occurred during the afternoon hours as a result of gusty winds from the initial thunderstorm.  During the afternoon and overnight hours of the 19th and 20th, 350 homes and 1500 people were evacuated. The fire burned 804 acres and destroyed 1 home along with several outbuildings and vehicles. The fire fuels were brush, dense rangeland grasses, and ponderosa pine. The fire was not completely extinguished until August 25th.|(At the time of this report, lost property values were not available.)

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front dropped across the area bringing in cold air. A Pacific storm then brought moisture in the form of heavy snow to much of Central and Southwest Montana. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eight inches of snow fell at Townsend 12E and 4 inches fell at Toston 13SW.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large spring storm associated with a cutoff upper level low, moved across the Northern Rockies on the afternoon of June 6th through the morning of June7th. Thunderstorms developed by early afternoon across northern Wyoming and southern Montana, with heavy rainfall beginning in Sheridan, Wyoming and Carbon County, Montana at 330pm and across much of southern Montana by 6pm. By 9pm Wednesday evening, heavy rainfall became more widespread across southern Montana and continued thruogh the overnight hours. By mid morning on the 6th, rainfall had ended across the impacted area. Runoff from areas upstream of Rosebud County and the Tongue River Reservoir resulted in flooding downstream of the dam.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three quarters of an inch of rain fell during the storm. Some minor branches were down by winds of 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front and upper level disturbance moved across the  central and eastern counties in the Billings County Warning Area during the late afternoon and evening hours of the 13th. Several thunderstorms developed across Northern Stillwater and Sweet Grass Counties before evolving into Supercell thunderstorms over Northern Yellowstone County. These thunderstorms then merged into a squall line across Eastern Yellowstone County and Treasure County before moving across Rosebud, Custer and Fallon Counties. Very large hail and damaging winds accompanied these thunderstorms with widespread damage reported across these areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous upper level storm system and strong cold front produced a widespread severe weather outbreak in northeastern Montana.  The majority of the events were high wind, a few reports of large hail, and two tornadoes.
Isolated severe weather occurred during the afternoon and evening hours across a portion of northeast Montana.

During the late afternoon and evening of June 16, 2007, a high precipitation supercell thunderstorm tracked from across northern Montana, just to the north of a warm front.  This was the most devastating hail storm to affect the area since at least 1999, and prompted 22 severe thunderstorm and 6 tornado warnings in the NWS Glasgow county warning area. The storm began early in the day just east of Glacier National Park and gradually moved hundreds of miles eastward through the day.  By 3 pm, the storm crossed into the NWS Glasgow area in Phillips County, where it started to make a turn to the southeast.   The storm traveled for over 250 miles before decreasing in Richland County near the North Dakota border a little after 11 pm.  Hail up to baseball size and wind gusts in excess of 85 mph accompanied the storm.  Torrential heavy rainfall was also experienced, prompting seven flash flood warnings to be issued.  The large hail left a hail swath seen on GOES and Terra MODIS satellites that had a path at least 225 miles long, and up to 12 miles wide. ||The most significant damage from the storm occurred in portions of Valley and McCone Counties.  The towns of Glasgow and Nashua, both in Valley County, took a direct hit from the storm.  In Glasgow, southeast winds gusting to 60 to 65 mph ahead of the storm switched into the northwest with gusts in excess of 70 mph.  Golfball to baseball sized hail fell for several minutes before diminishing to penny sized for another 10-20 minutes.  The majority of the homes and businesses in Glasgow had some sort of damage including:  broken windows, damaged shingles, dented or broken gutters and skirting.  Many vehicles also had significant hail damage.  Just the property damage alone in Glasgow was nearly $8.0 million dollars.  As the storm continued east it hit the town of Nashua and every home and business had some form of damage from the baseball size hail and the damaging winds that occurred with it.   The winds were measured at 89 mph just west of town.  Property damage was estimated to be $3 million dollars. | |In rural areas outside of Glasgow and Nashua there were several cottonwood trees completely uprooted.  Horses and cattle were injured by the hail and wind, and Montana Fish, Wildlife and Parks reported many birds and small animals were killed by the hail.  There were thousands of acres of alfalfa, wheat, and corn that were completely destroyed.  At this time, damage estimates to crops in Valley County are at $20 million dollars.||The storm also produced heavy rainfall of 1 to 2 inches, and was followed later in the evening by additional heavy rain.  Rainfall of 2 to 7 inches fell in the Antelope, Brazil and Willow Creek basins.  The Antelope Creek basin had its most significant flood on record and one road was completely underwater for nearly a third of a mile.  One ranch was isolated by the flood waters for three days. ||As the storm continued across northern McCone County and into western Richland County it continued to produce hail up to the size of ping pong balls and wind gusts that were estimated as high as 80 to 90 mph knocked down telephone and electrical poles.   There was significant damage to farms and ranches in the Vida area, as well as significant loss of crops, estimated at $5 million dollars.  The rural nature of the area minimized the property damage that could have occurred.  ||The overall estimated damage in this storm between the hail and wind damage, and the subsequent flooding is 34.2 million dollars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Windows broken out on the west side of a house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On April 17, a strong negatively tilted short wave trough with favorable wind shear aloft moved across Southwest Montana. Strong to severe thunderstorms developed along the surface front and tracked northeast across the area. These thunderstorms were hail producers.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major severe weather episode occurred from mid-afternoon on the 2nd through the pre-dawn hours of the 3rd.  A very moist and unstable southwest flow aloft was responsible for the severe weather. Although the majority of the reports were of either large hail or strong wind gusts, there were a few spots that had flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail destroyed a kitchen window.
